skos:Concept.skos:scopeNote: Factual information conveyed orally to a recipient. Where this has been transcribed, use an appropriate term from the TEXT hierarchy.
General term for images used to represent a particular place or item, or convey information.
A visual representation of a physical object, place or person. [DC] Use a more specific term where appropriate.
An image created manually using paint or pigments, to record, illustrate or convey impressions.
An image used to convey information, often as part of a larger publication or exhibition.
Reproduced image derived by mechanical printing from an image engraved or carved on a suitable surface.
Card printed with an image, often a photograph on one side, and space for a short message and a postal address on the other.
A manually produced image for illustration or record purposes.
A drawing, often made in the field as the basis for later work, or to quickly convey important information.
A thin opaque, slightly absorbent material, generally made from processing of plant or other fibres, for use as a medium for writing, drawing and printing.
Item in a format suitable for normal reading or viewing, without a computer or other device. For digital files or disks use MACHINE READABLE STORAGE MEDIUM or specific terms.

A service is a system that provides one or more functions of value to the end-user. Examples include: a photocopying service, a banking service, an authentication service, inter-library loans, a Z39.50 or Web server. [DC]
A database stored on a computer linked to the internet, which can be queried and browsed by remote users using the internet.
A book containing information about a place that has been produced for the use of visitors or tourists.
Short publication used to disseminate information about a particular project or organisation, issued on a regular basis.
A written work issued at regular intervals, numbered consecutively with no intended or planned end point.
A bound written work.
A short published document, often a single sheet, describing a particular place, feature or service to assist visitors and users.
General term for documents that have been produced in large numbers for public sale or distribution.
Publication on a specific topic, or limited scope of subject matter. Complete in itself, though may form part of a series.
A resource whose content is primarily words for reading. For example - books, letters, dissertations, poems, newspapers, articles, archives of mailing lists. [DC]
A written work for publication, being one of several found in a serial, monograph or magazine.
A written work issued in parts, with the intention of creating a completed sequence.
The class or material that a photograph is produced upon. [AAT] Use a specific term or terms as appropriate.
A sheet of glass coated in a light sensitive substance on which a photgraphic image can be developed.
Thin round flexible disc with magnetic surfaces for storing and retrieving data. [AAT]
Storage medium for information (text, images, files etc) in an encoded digital format.
The storage of material (text, images, files etc) in an electromagnetic format.
Data storage device in disc form that uses lasers to write and access data; can store data in analog or digital form. [AAT]
A disc capable of holding more data than a conventional CD or CD-Rom. Commonly used as storage medium for film and interactive products.
Optical discs on which programs, data, or music are digitally encoded for a laser beam to scan, decode and transmit to a playback system, computer monitor or television set. [AAT]
Data stored in a format that is meant to be manipulated and read in one or more software systems.
A broad class of drawing that is produced following precise conventions of scale and projection. [AAT]
A drawing used to convey information about the arrangement and size of features in a defined location under study.
A full written presentation of the details of a particular event or investigative technique, often including illustrations and appendices.
A report detailing the results of archaeological fieldwork that has been produced in advance of a full synthesis of the collected data.
A photographic slide of a black and white (monochromatic) image.
A map generally produced manually to indicate main features of an area quickly, for example to assist location of a particular place.
A pictoral depiction of an area of landscape.
Cards, often printed with forms, for the recording and sorting of information in a structured manner.
A structured set of data taken from a larger work to assist in location of particular subject matter.
A paper form used to record specific information in a systematic way.

A form used to record the details of a feature identified by archaeological investigation, generally including location, depth, colour, texture, inclusions and stratigraphic relationship to other contexts.
A written extract taken from a published newspaper.
Plan showing a horizontal section, size and arrangement of rooms within a building, may include location of doors, windows and other features.
A drawn representation of an archaeological 'find' or other cultural object.
A book in which visitors to a property, monument or exhibition record their thoughts and impressions on their experience.
A photograph taken from the air.

An aerial photograph taken from directly above a feature in the landscape, suitable for mapping or survey purposes. For photographs taken from the air at an angle to the subject use OBLIQUE AERIAL PHOTOGRAPH.

An image created by controlled exposure of light sensitive film. Usually a static image in time and space. For digital photographs double index with an appropriate term under MACHINE READABLE STORAGE MEDIUM.
An aerial photograph taken at an angle other than 90 degrees to a feature in the landscape.
A diary used to record the events and activities during an excavation.
A chronological journal, generally showing appointments, or recording memories and impressions of a days events over a period of time.
An outline of the circumstances to be addressed, with an indication of the scope of work which will be required (English term)
A film strip or roll containing reduced images.
A medium containing microphotographic images, usually reproduced on film, requiring magnification to render them readable.
Used for orthographic drawings in the horizontal plane, generally from above generally depicts localised areas.
A report detailing the results of an archaeological event were the ground surface has been studied and any visible archaeological material collected.
A hard copy of digital data that has been printed onto paper.
Information encoded in a defined structure (for example, lists, tables, and databases), intended to be useful for direct machine processing. [DC] For manual data use index, gazetteer etc.
Data suitable for processing using database management software.
A photographic frame of developed colour film which can be used to print a photographic image.
An outline of the circumstances to be addressed, with an indication of the scope of work which will be required (Scottish term)
A form that records the details of a project, for example, an excavation index form.
Recorded interview intended to preserve the recollections of persons whose experiences or memories are representative of historical life.
A sound is a resource whose content is primarily intended to be rendered as audio. [DC] For example recorded speech, sounds or music.
A positive black and white image on film which can be projected onto a screen when light is shone through it.
A photographic print of a polychrome image (composed of colours other than, but not exclusive of, black, white or grey tones).
A report depicting the results generated from an intensive survey of a large area of land including field recording, measured site surveys and land history.
A resource comprising of a group of items the parts of which may be separately described and navigated. [DC]
Documents relating to the administration of planning applications.
Broad term for all types of animated or live action images.
An extract from a longer video film, used as an illustration.
Recording of a complete broadcast television programme, for instance a documentary. For extracts such as items from news broadcasts use VIDEO CLIP.
A report detailing the results of a study of the fabric of a standing building.
Structured list of information about items of interest to a research project, for example monuments of a particular type.
Structured descriptions of an archive or collection used to assist retrieval and management, or presentation to the public.
An image produced by creating marks on a hard material from which a print is then taken.
Written communication, generally in the form of letters.
A photograph usually of a standing structure that has been manipulated to allow the creation of a measured drawing of the facade or elevation.
Any material that records, holds and communicates information in a primarily visual manner.
Positive image on continuous film strip suitable for projection of moving images.
Recording of a complete broadcast radio programme.
A flexible, transparent sheet of film bearing a number of reduced images.
A schematic depiction of the results of an archaeological excavation.
Drawing of the vertical face of an excavated feature or features depicting the vertical extent and stratigraphic relationship between identified layers or deposits.
Machine readable files suitable for use in a geographic information system (GIS).

A schedule of work set out in sufficient detail for it to be quantifiable, implemented and monitored. Can include contractual details relevant to the commissioning body. A project design may be a research proposal independent of the planning framework
A report detailing the results of a field survey. Usually restricted to a survey of a small or specific area or site.
A heavy grade, water proof plastic film used for the preparation of technical drawings.

This term is appropriate to official notifications of a monument or area whether they are statutory or purely advisory. Where possible the specific document type should be indexed for example Scheduled Monument Notification.
The contents of these documents do not confer any statutory designation on the monuments listed, they are purely advisory notifications for use within preserving the historic environment.

A list of English battlefields maintained by English Heritage. Inclusion in the document does not confer any statutory status. It lists and records the details of battlefields to preserve their nature and survival.

A register which including sites retaining any evidence of pre-1750 layouts; significant evidence of pre-1820 layouts; good examples of the best gardens from 1820 to 1880; are good examples of the best gardens laid out after 1880 but before 1970.
A resource which requires interaction from the user to be understood, executed, or experienced. [DC]
Materials selected and organised to be of use to teachers for use in education.
A file of computer information stored on a computer connected to the internet coded so that it can be accessed over the internet using browser software.
A report which represents an initial synthesis of the data collected from an excavation ahead of the production of a full publication report.
The process of transcribing the features on an aerial photograph onto a map or plan.
A photographic reproduction of written or printed material produced by using toner particles under heat and pressure to fuse a copy onto blank paper.
A form to record the details of a monument or site found during the course of a field survey or visit.
A photographic frame of developed black and white film from which a positive print can be produced.
This class of documents confers statutory designation on the monuments listed within them. It usually is restricted to legally defined national or local notifications of designation.

Any notification, update or amendment relating to the scheduling of a monument. Usually includes description and a map showing the limit of the scheduling. It confers a statutory status and receipt is an acknowledgement of the scheduling.

A list of buildings of special architectural or historic interest. This list confers a statutory level of protection status to the buildings contained within it and usually includes a description of each building.
A report that primarily details the results of attempts to assign a date to materials or deposits using scientific methods such as Palaeomagnetism, Dendrochronology or Radiocarbon Dating.
A report that primarily details the results of attempts to assign a date to orgainic material by measuring the amount of carbon 14 it contains.
Collection of artefacts recovered during archaeological investigation of a site.
A report detailing the results of an archaeological evaluation
A schematic diagram showing the relationships of the composition and strata of layers identified during excavation or intervention.
A translucent paper, which when laid over an existing image, permits the image to be seen and/or traced.
Magnetic tape used to record sound. Includes both cassette and reel tapes.
A representation of a monument or landscape based on historical or archaeological information.
A positive colour image on film which can be projected onto a screen when light is shone through it.
A photographic slide of a colour (polychromatic) image.
A report detailing the archaeological and architectural potential of a site or locality.
A schedule of work in sufficient detail for it to be quantifiable, implemented and monitored.
A report written as a result of archaeological monitoring during development work.
A scaled drawing based on measurements taken from life.
Written copy of recorded interviews collected to illustrate history from personal experiences.
An unpublished document.
A collection of items presented in a protective folder.
A report that primarily details the results of attempts to assign a date to wooden structures or objects by the comparative study of the growth rings in trees and aged wood.
A drawing which depicts to scale a feature within the landscape. For drawings of selected features of a site use SCALE DRAWING..
A report detailing the results of an archaeological excavation.
Paper made for writing or drawing underwater.
Book used to record information or impressions in the field, often as a preparation for more formal reporting at a later date.
Data suitable for processing using computer aided design (CAD) packages.
Photographic film responsive to infrared radiation. Used to record temperature variations in the subject, indicative of hidden archaeological or architectural features.
A relief process in which the design is cut into and printed from the plank side of a wood block [AAT].
A drawing, generally on a transparent sheet, which can be placed on top of another image to combine information from both.
A magnetic tape used to record moving images and sound. Use for both cassette and reel tapes.
A depiction of land, coastal or sea areas intended to aid navigation, usually using latitude and longitude co-ordinates.
A plan of a feature or small area generally done in the field to quickly record the main features.
A report that primarily details the results of a survey that was conducted using remote sensing techniques such as resistivity and magnetometry.
Scale drawing showing the horizontal extent, height and arrangement of an excavated layer or feature on an archaeological site.
A photographic print of a mononchrome image (composed of grey, black and white tones).
A report on the archaeology of an area, based primarily on existing records and resources.
Organised and documented collection of the records made during the course of a project.
